info.parenttitle: S3.1 The periodic table: Classification of elements

markdown:
## Trends Across a Period and Down a Group


All periodic trends can be explained by **two key factors**:

1. **Effective Nuclear Charge ($Z_\text{eff}$):**
  1.  Higher $Z_\text{eff}$ **pulls electrons closer**, decreasing atomic and ionic radii, and increasing ionization energy and electronegativity.
2. **Shielding Effect:**
  1. Inner electrons **shield outer electrons** from the nucleus, reducing $Z_\text{eff}$ and making it easier to remove or add electrons.

<definition term="Effective nuclear charge">**Effective nuclear charge** is the net positive charge experienced by valence electrons.</definition>

![Diagram showing inner electrons shielding the nucleus from a valence electron.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/8b20a4ed9823d5bec9e0bc613bf7dd16f6ac4cf0-391x364.png)

## Atomic Radius: The Size of an Atom


<definition term="Atomic radius">The **atomic radius** is the distance from the nucleus of an atom to the outermost electron.</definition>

While we cannot measure this directly (since electrons exist in a cloud), we **approximate** it based on the distances between two bonded atoms.

### **Trend Across a Period**


1. As you move **across a period from left to right**, the atomic radius **decreases**. 
2. Each successive element adds a proton to the nucleus and an electron to the same energy level. 
3. This increases the **effective nuclear charge ($Z_\text{eff}$), **pulling the electrons **closer to the nucleus**.

<callout type="example">* Consider **sodium** (Na) and **chlorine** (Cl), both in Period 3. 
* Sodium has an atomic radius of 186 pm, while chlorine’s radius is only 99 pm. 
* The additional protons in chlorine's nucleus exert a stronger pull on its electrons, shrinking the atomic radius.</callout>

![Atomic radius decreases from sodium to chlorine across Period 3.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5c128816ff7383c85d680375016a13529bcf2dec-770x235.png)

### **Trend Down a Group**


1. As you move **down a group**, the atomic radius **increases**. 
2. This is because each successive element adds a **new energy level** (or shell), increasing the distance between the nucleus and the outermost electrons.
3. Although the nuclear charge also increases, the additional inner shells provide **shielding**, **reducing the pull on the valence electrons**.

<callout type="analogy">* Think of the nucleus as a magnet and the electrons as metal balls. 
* Adding more layers of **insulation** (energy levels) **weakens the magnet’s pull **on the outermost balls.</callout>

![Group 1 elements with electron configurations showing atomic radius increases from Li to Rb.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/71a4f47547d69b61623f8f702ef4f81f640fed18-452x323.png)

## Ionic Radius: The Size of Ions


<definition term="Ionic radius">**Ionic radius** is the measure of an ion's size, defined as the distance from its nucleus to the outermost electron, influenced by its charge and electron configuration.</definition>

> **Cations **(positively charged ions) are **smaller **than their parent atoms, while **anions **(negatively charged ions) are **larger**.

### **Trend Across a Period**


1. For **cations**, the **ionic radius decreases across a period **due to increasing nuclear charge. 
2. For **anions**, the **same trend holds**, but anions are **always larger than cations** within the same period.

<callout type="example">Consider $Na^{+}$ (cation) and $Cl^{-}$ (anion).

* $Na^{+}$ has lost its outermost electron, reducing electron-electron repulsion and shrinking its radius to 102 pm. 
* $Cl^{-}$, however, has gained an electron, increasing repulsion and expanding its radius to 181 pm.</callout>

![Graph of ionic radius for selected period 3 ions from Na plus to Cl minus.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/d618181fcdbad81d739113700d24e8e3c9105a27-595x369.png)

### **Trend Down a Group**


**Ionic radius increases down a group for both cations and anions**, following the same reasoning as atomic radius (additional energy levels and shielding).

## Ionic Radius for Isoelectronic Ions


### **What Are Isoelectronic Ions?**


<definition term="Isoelectronic ions">**Isoelectronic ions** are ions that have the same number of electrons but different nuclear charges.</definition>

Despite having the same electron configuration, their ionic radii vary due to differences in **nuclear attraction** on the electron cloud.

### **Trend Across Isoelectronic Series**


1. In a series of isoelectronic ions (e.g., $O^{2-}$, $F^-$, $Na^+$, $Mg^{2+}$), the ionic radius decreases as nuclear charge increases.g., $O^{2-}$, $F^-$, $Na^+$, $Mg^{2+}$), the ionic radius decreases as nuclear charge increases. 
2. This is because a **greater positive charge** pulls the electrons **closer to the nucleus**, **reducing the radius**.

<callout type="example">* $O^{2-}$ has the largest radius due to its lower nuclear charge.
* $Mg^{2+}$ has the smallest radius as its higher nuclear charge pulls electrons more tightly.</callout>

![Comparison of the radii of a sodium atom and a sodium cation.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/e34d844e7c11f017e3873d3155e9082839694bb9-671x243.png)

![Comparison of the radii of a chlorine atom and a chloride ion, showing the ion is larger.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3c4c6ecaad61f2f00f79605a1818d8d225df6cd3-712x287.png)

### **Key Factors Affecting Isoelectronic Radius:**


1. **Nuclear Charge:** Higher positive charge reduces ionic radius.
2. **Electron Repulsion:** More negative ions experience greater electron repulsion, increasing radius.

<callout type="warning">* Students often assume all isoelectronic ions have the same radius since they have the same number of electrons. 
* However, the **difference in nuclear charge** affects the size significantly.</callout>

## Ionization Energy: The Energy to Remove an Electron


<definition term="Ionization energy">**Ionization energy** ($IE$) is the minimum energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom in its ground state.</definition>

It can be expressed as:

$$X^{n+}(g)  \to X^{(n+1)+} (g) + e^-$$

### **Trend Across a Period**


1. Ionization energy **increases **across a period. 
2. As the effective nuclear charge increases, electrons are held more **tightly**, requiring more energy to remove one.

<callout type="example">* The first ionization energy of sodium is $496 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, while that of chlorine is $1251 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. 
* Chlorine’s **higher nuclear charge** makes it **harder to remove an electron**.</callout>

### **Trend Down a Group**


1. Ionization energy **decreases **down a group. 
2. The outermost electrons are farther from the nucleus and experience **greater shielding**, making them easier to remove.

<callout type="tip">**Smaller** atoms hold their electrons **more tightly**, so they have **higher ionization energies**.</callout>

<callout type="warning">* Many students think ionization energy decreases across a period because atoms get heavier. 
* This is incorrect: nuclear charge, not mass, determines the trend.</callout>

![Graph of first ionization energy, in kJ mol⁻1, versus atomic number for elements H to Ca showing periodic peaks at noble gases and troughs at alkali metals.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3849234c283156164848292acf9bc497694b86ab-765x497.png)

## Electron Affinity: The Energy of Gaining an Electron


<definition term="Electron affinity">**Electron affinity** (EA) is the energy change when an atom gains an electron. A more negative value indicates a stronger attraction for the electron.</definition>

$$X(g) + e^- \to X^-(g)$$

### **Trend Across a Period**


1. Electron affinity becomes more **exothermic **(more negative) across a period.
2. As **nuclear charge **increases, atoms more readily attract additional electrons.

<callout type="example">* **Chlorine** has an electron affinity of $-349 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, meaning it releases a large amount of energy when gaining an electron. 
* **Sodium’s** EA, in contrast, is only $-53 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.</callout>

### **Trend Down a Group**


1. Electron affinity becomes **less exothermic** (less negative) down a group.
2. Increased **shielding **and distance from the nucleus reduce the attraction for an added electron.

<callout type="note">Some elements, such as noble gases, have electron affinities that are near zero or not strongly exothermic because adding an electron is unfavourable for their stable electron configurations.</callout>

## Electronegativity: The Pull in a Bond


<definition term="Electronegativity ">**Electronegativity **is the ability of an atom to attract shared electrons in a covalent bond.</definition>

> It is a dimensionless value, with fluorine being the most electronegative element (3.98 on the Pauling scale).

### **Trend Across a Period**


1. Electronegativity **increases **across a period as atomic radius decreases and nuclear charge increases. 
2. Smaller atoms with higher nuclear charge pull bonding electrons more strongly.

<callout type="example">**Fluorine** (Period 2) is more electronegative than oxygen because it has a smaller radius and higher effective nuclear charge.</callout>

### **Trend Down a Group**


1. Electronegativity **decreases **down a group. 
2. Larger atoms with more shielding are less able to attract bonding electrons.

<callout type="tip">* **Electronegativity** trends are similar to** ionization energy** trends. 
* When in doubt, compare the two!</callout>
